rulings.Case Title: CONSERVATORSHIP OF KIM SENG TANG
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #1, Report, Fees
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 12/15/25. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) No courtesy copy of the third supplement, filed on 9/25/25 has been received yet by the examiner. It was partially reviewed online, and it appears that an amendment will need to be filed. (2) Court-conformed accountings in Probate matters are cash, not accrual, accountings (using the appraised valued and carry values going forward). There is no such thing as "Gains/Appreciation." If the market value of assets has increased, this must be shown on an additional schedule. [Probate Code Section 1063(a).] (3) Schedule E, in other words, should have BOTH columns: estimated market value and carry value. (4) The accounting now reportedly begins on 5/4/23 and ends on 7/31/24, but the first Marriott International statement is the one dated 10/12/23. [Probate Code Section 2620(c).] Deadline to provide the assigned examiner courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues is 12/1/25. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF EUGENE ARMISTEAD CARTER
rulings.Calendar Matter: Respondents Victoria Beuerle And Michael Paul Carter'S Notice Of Demurrer And Demurrer To Petitioner Eddie Caboara'S Third Amended Petition To Set Aside The Order For Final Distribution; For Return Of Property; Conversion; Breach Of Fiduciary Duty; Constructive Fraud; Intentional Interference With Expected Inheritance; Double Damages; And Attorney'S Fees
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 3] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the court is Respondents Victoria Beuerle's and Michael Paul Carter's Demurrer to Petitioner Eddie Caboara's Third Amended Petition to Set Aside the Order for Final Distribution; For Return of Property; Conversion; Breach of Fiduciary Duty; Constructive Fraud; Intentional Interference with Expected Inheritance; Double Damages; and Attorney Fees ("TAP"). Respondents' request for judicial notice of certain documents is GRANTED, pursuant to Evid. Code section 452. Demurrer is OVERRULED IN PART AND SUSTAINED IN PART, WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. Per the Notice of Demurrer, Respondents demur to (1) the causes of action for intentional interference with inheritance; breach of fiduciary duty pursuant to Probate Code sec. 850; and abuse of dependent adult, on the basis that Petitioner's paternity has not been established and thus these causes of action are not yet ripe and fail to state a claim on which relief can be granted; (2) the cause of action for return of estate property, double damages, and attorney's fees pursuant to Probate Code sections 850 et seq and 859, on the basis that the TAP does not allege that Decedent held an enforceable claim under section 850 during his lifetime, as required by the statute. Separately, Respondents demur to the section 859 claim on the additional basis that the TAP does not plead facts alleging bad faith, but merely states Respondents acted in bad faith in wrongfully taking or disposing of property belonging to the estate. (3) the cause of action for abuse of a dependent adult as a basis for damages under Probate Code section 859, on the basis that the TAP does not plead facts establishing that Petitioner is a dependent adult pursuant to Welf. & Inst. Code section 15610.23. In the Memorandum of Points and Authorities, Respondents demur to the cause of action for intentional interference with inheritance on the basis that, although it is listed in the TAP's caption, it is not pleaded as a specific cause of action in the body of the TAP. A demurrer is a pleading used to challenge the legal sufficiency of an opponent's pleading, based on defects that appear either on the face of the pleading under attack or from matters outside the pleading that are judicially noticeable. (See Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318; County of Fresno v. Shelton (1998) 66 Cal.App.4th 996, 1008, 1009.) A ruling on a demurrer does not determine contested factual issues relating to the merits of the claims.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF EUGENE ARMISTEAD CARTER
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For The Purspose Of Posting Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 3] Demurrer to causes of action on the basis that Petitioner's paternity claim has not yet been judicially determined is OVERRULED. The court has taken judicial notice of the records and documents submitted by Respondents, pursuant to Evid. Code section 452. Judicial notice is limited to the relevant facts properly subject to notice, given the materials presented and does not, for example, extend to the truth of assertions in those materials. (See Richtek USA, Inc. v. UPI Semiconductor Corp. (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 651, 659-660; Evid. C. section 450 et seq.) "A mistaken notion exists that taking judicial notice of court records means noticing " '... the existence of facts asserted in [all the documents in] a court file ... a court cannot take judicial notice of hearsay allegations as being true, just because they are part of a court record or file....' " (Garcia v. Sterling (1985) 176 Cal. App. 3d 17, 21-22, quoting Day v. Sharp (1975) 50 Cal.App.3d 904, 914, emphasis in original.) Here, although the submitted documents' existence and contents have been judicially noticed, a demurrer is not the appropriate context in which to determine contested paternity claims. When considering a demurrer, the trial court must accept as true all material facts pleaded in the complaint and those arising by reasonable implication. (Moore v. Conliffe (1994) 7 Cal. 4th 634, 638; Montclair Parkowners Ass'n v. City of Montclair (1999) 76 Cal. App. 4th 784.; Sheehan v. San Francisco 49ers, Ltd. (2009) Cal.4th 992, 998.) Petitioner alleges throughout the TAP he is Decedent's son. Demurrer to cause of action for intentional interference with inheritance on the basis of failure to plead the elements of the cause of action is SUSTAINED, with leave to amend. Demurrer to cause of action for return of estate property, double damages, and attorney's fees pursuant to Probate Code sections 850 et seq and 859, on the basis that the TAP does not allege that Decedent held an enforceable claim under section 850 during his lifetime, is SUSTAINED, with leave to amend. Demurrer to the TAP's section 859 claim for damages, on the additional basis that the TAP does not plead facts alleging "a person has in bad faith wrongfully taken, concealed, or disposed of property belonging to a conservatee, a minor, an elder, a dependent adult, a trust, or the estate of a decedent, or has taken, concealed, or disposed of the property by the use of undue influence in bad faith or through the commission of elder or dependent adult financial abuse, as defined in Section 15610.30 of the Welfare and Institutions Code," but merely gives the conclusory statement at paragraph 60 of the TAP that "Respondents in bad faith wrongfully took, or disposed of, property belonging to the estate, and are therefore liable for twice the value of the property taken and, in the Court's discretion, Petitioner's attorney's fees and costs pursuant to Probate Code [sec.] 859" is SUSTAINED, with leave to amend. Demurrer to the cause of action for abuse of a dependent adult, on the basis that the TAP does not plead facts establishing that Petitioner is a dependent adult pursuant to Welf. & Inst. Code section 15610.23 is SUSTAINED, with leave to amend. Petitioner Eddie Caboara may file a further amended petition, within ten (10) days after entry of this Order.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF LEONISA TRIAS
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #1, Report
rulings.Rulings: Hearing. Appearance required, as nothing has been filed to address the following issues (e-mailed to counsel on 05/27/2025): 1) Item 1 of the DE-120 form must be checked. New notice must be given. 2) The decedent's life insurance policies and burial plot have not been inventoried and appraised. 3) Was the estate the designated beneficiary of each life insurance policy? If so, please explain why the estate had not received any proceeds at the end of this account period. 4) Please explain why the estate cash has not been kept in interest-bearing accounts since December 2022. 5) Please explain the nature and purpose of the $100 disbursement to US Bank on 12/30/2022. 6) Did the estate pay a late penalty and fees for the 2nd installment of the 2022-2023 property taxes? The due date was 04/10/2023. The amount due was $770.43, but the schedule of disbursements listed $892.47 paid on 04/12/2023. 7) Did Prestina Macias, Carlos Macias, and/or other occupants of the estate real property make payments to maintain the property (mortgage, property taxes, insurance, utilities, etc.) during this account period, to the extent not reflected on the schedule of disbursements? 8) Please explain why the schedule of liabilities did not list the following liens on the estate real property: i) Mortgage; and ii) $300,000 lien in favor of Prestina Macias and Carlos Macinas described in the court-approved October 2021 settlement agreement. 9) Petitioner's bond is insufficient based on the total value of the estate personal property on hand at the end of this account period ($35,000). 10) No financial account statement has been filed to verify the ending cash balance. LRSF 14.34(D). A declaration re retention of the original statement required by LRSF 14.59(A) & CRC 2.257 must also be filed. PARTIES APPEARING SHOULD CHECK IN FOR THE HEARING WITH THEIR FULL NAME. Counsel and parties may appear personally or remotely through Zoom or CourtCall. For a video appearance, go to zoom.us, click "join a meeting" and input meeting ID 160 225 4765 and password 514879. rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF KINUKO ARIIZUMI JORGENSEN
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting, Final Distribution
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 12/15/25. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) The title of each pleading and of each proposed order must clearly and completely identify the nature of the relief sought or granted. [CRC 7.102.] (2) The petition does not meet all the requirements of CRC 7.550. It does not list the creditors' claims, and the information about sales, purchases, or exchanges of assets should disclose the amount of sale proceeds for any sold assets. (3) The petition does not set forth the complete legal description and assessor's parcel number of the real property to be distributed. [CRC. 7.651(b).] (The value of the real property is unnecessary.) (4) Paragraph 19 of the petition indicates that the attorney is to be reimbursed $1,763.00, but the prayer requests the administrator be allowed to reimburse himself $1,978.00. Who is to be reimbursed and what amount? (5) The net cash to be distributed to the sole heir is shown on Exhibit C to be $287,168.38, but the proposed order shows a net balance of $286,953.38? (6) The initial Petition for Letters of Administration filed on 9/15/22 stated that the decedent died in the City and County of San Francisco. Her death certificate shows that she died in San Leandro. Please provide a revised proposed order and courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues to the assigned examiner by 12/1/25. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.

rulings.Case Title: CONSERVATORSHIP OF SHARON CARNEY
rulings.Calendar Matter: ACCOUNTING PETITION RE: Accounting #1, Report, Fees
rulings.Rulings: Continued to 12/15/25. (No earlier availability.) Issue(s) necessitating continuance: (1) A closer look at the list attached to Edward Allen Mueller's declaration shows 14K gold earrings sold for $178 and an 18K gold crown signet ring sold for $287. Are these the pieces inventoried as J013 and J016? (2) Why was there so much damage in the apartment in just 6-7 months from the time Diana Tate-Andretich entered and took photos (and apparently did not detect the odor of cat urine?) and the time Ed went to pick up the items? Did anyone else in the apartment building notice rodents? Did Conservator, who noticed rat droppings, not notify the building manager? The photographs of the empty apartment (Exhibit C) do not appear to evidence widespread damage to the floors as one might expect from so much water damage, cat urine, and a rodent infestation. At least two of the pieces of antique furniture, the bottoms of which reportedly had "rotted out", appear in the photos with items in or on them. How did the bottoms rot out so completely within 7 months that the furniture had to be disposed of? (3) To whom did Ed donate the tangible personal property he did not dispose of or keep to sell? Is the estate not entitled to claim any credit for charitable donations of donated furniture and artwork since they were all part of the "package" of items Ed was permitted to sell or keep (or discard or donate)? (4) Were the two cousins contacted, who requested at the outset of the proceedings that they be involved in closing out the apartment? They wanted to ensure that family heirlooms don't get discarded. (5) Did Ed get a receipt from the neighbor for the wood walnut armoire? How was the rug intended for Tom Tavis not destroyed by water damage, cat urine, or rodents? (6) Did the probate referee not look at the tangible personal property himself after he received the appraisal from Estate Liaison Services? Tangible personal property worth $15,000 is not insignificant. Please provide courtesy copies of any papers filed to address these issues to the assigned examiner by 12/1/25. Examiner continuances are final and not subject to contest under LRSF 14.10 B.

[See CRC 7.3 for definitions.])
